file_name: EMERALDHEALTHTHERAPEUTICSINC_06_10_2020-EX-4.5-CONSULTING AGREEMENT - DR. GAETANO MORELLO N.D. INC..txt

perturbation.original_text: 3.3 Deductions and Remittances. Emerald shall not be obliged to deduct or retain from the Fees due to the Contractor, nor shall it be obliged to remit same to the required governmental authority, any amounts that may be required by law or regulation to be deducted, retained and remitted including, without limitation, Federal and Provincial or State Income Tax, Workers' Compensation and Pension Plan deductions and remittances. All such remittances and other payments are entirely the responsibility of the Contractor and the Contractor hereby indemnifies and saves Emerald and its Board members and officers harmless from any liability of any kind whatsoever that they may incur as a result of the Contractor's failure to make such remittances or payments.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 Deductions and Remittances. Emerald shall not be obliged to deduct or retain from the Fees due to the Contractor, nor shall it be obliged to remit same to the required governmental authority, any amounts that may be required by law or regulation to be deducted, retained and remitted including, without limitation, Federal and Provincial or State Income Tax, Workers' Compensation and Pension Plan deductions and remittances. All such remittances and other payments are entirely the responsibility of the Contractor unless the Contractor is later determined to be an employee by a relevant authority. In such case, Emerald will be liable for deductions, remittances and associated penalties that were not withheld during the contract. The Contractor hereby indemnifies and saves Emerald and its Board members and officers harmless from any liability of any kind whatsoever that they may incur as a result of the Contractor's failure to make such remittances or payments, unless the Contractor is deemed to be an employee.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contingency where if the contractor is later deemed an employee, Emerald could be liable for deductions and remittances they didn't make, thus conflicting with the initial statement that all such payments are 'entirely the responsibility of the contractor'.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the spirit of the Canada Labour Code which mandates employer responsibilities for deductions and remittances if an employer-employee relationship is found, regardless of contractual wording.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: labour standards - Canada.ca labour standards Page details Date modified: 2017-04-26
perturbation.justification: The original text clearly states that the contractor is entirely responsible for all remittances and payments. The modified text introduces a contingency where Emerald could be liable for these payments if the contractor is later deemed an employee. This directly contradicts the initial statement of complete contractor responsibility. The Law Explanation suggests that this change contradicts the spirit of the Canada Labour Code, which mandates employer responsibilities. The perturbation explanation also confirms that the modified clause introduces a direct conflict with the original agreement, potentially exposing Emerald to liabilities that it explicitly disclaimed in the original contract. This constitutes a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction because the contract attempts to define responsibilities in a way that deviates from potential legal classifications of the contractor's status.

perturbation.original_text: 2.5 Not Employment. The parties acknowledge and agree that the relationship created by operation of this Agreement is not an employment relationship.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.5 Not Employment. The parties acknowledge and agree that the relationship created by operation of this Agreement is not an employment relationship, unless a court determines otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'unless a court determines otherwise' introduces uncertainty about the contractor's status. The original text explicitly states it's not an employment relationship, while the addendum implies it might be under certain circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principles - Determining Employee vs. Contractor Status
perturbation.law_citation: Various Common Law Cases (e.g., Sagaz Industries Canada Inc. v. 6711221 Canada Inc., 2001 SCC 20)
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct statute, common law principles (developed through court cases) dictate factors for determining employment status. This clause creates ambiguity, potentially conflicting with legal precedents by initially denying an employment relationship, then implying the courts could override it. The court determines the status based on the facts and not necessarily on the wording of the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: common law | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you common law Common law is law that is derived from judicial decisions instead of from statutes . American courts originally fashioned common law rules based on English common law until the American legal system was sufficiently mature to create common law rules either from direct precedent or by analogy to comparable areas of decided law. In the 2019 Supreme Court case of Gamble v. United States , Justice Thomas issued a concurring opinion discussing common law and, in particular, the role of stare decisis in a common law system. Though most common law is found at the state level, there is a limited body of federal common law--that is, rules created and applied by federal courts absent any controlling federal statute. In the 2020 Supreme Court opinion Rodriguez v. FDIC , a unanimous Court quoted an earlier decision to explain that federal "common lawmaking must be 'necessary to protect uniquely federal interests'" in striking down a federal common law rule addressing the distribution of corporate tax refunds. At the state level, legislatures often subsequently codify common law rules from the courts of their state, either to give the rule the permanence afforded by a statute, to modify it somehow (by either expanding or restricting the scope of the common law rule, for example) or to replace the outcome entirely with legislation. An example that gained national attention was the 2018 California Supreme Court decision in Dynamex Operations West, Inc. v. Superior Court , which articulated a three-part test for determining whether California workers were independent contractors or employees for purposes of California labor law. The California Legislature responded by creating a new section of the Labor Code, 2750.3 , which codified and expanded on the Dynamex holding and went into effect on January 1, 2020. Note that, like many statutes responding to a common law rule, California Labor Code Section 2750.3 specifically mentions the Dynamex holding. [Last reviewed in May of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS legal history CIVICS THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal law criminal procedure statutory interpretation wex definitions civil procedure government Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contingency ('unless a court determines otherwise') that undermines the original clear statement that the agreement does 'not' create an employment relationship. This contradicts the common law principle, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 1, where courts determine employment status based on facts, potentially overriding the contract's initial denial of an employment relationship. The added clause creates ambiguity and directly challenges the enforceability of the original statement, which aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 Although the Contractor is being hired as an independent contractor to Emerald, it is acknowledged and agreed that the Contractor will generally best promote the interests of Emerald by being seconded, or providing material advice and support, to one or more of Emerald's subsidiaries, Affiliates or associates (the "Portfolio Companies").
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 Although the Contractor is being hired as an independent contractor to Emerald, it is acknowledged and agreed that the Contractor will generally best promote the interests of Emerald by being seconded, or providing direction and control, to one or more of Emerald's subsidiaries, Affiliates or associates (the "Portfolio Companies").
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'material advice and support' to 'direction and control' implies a level of authority and oversight over the Portfolio Companies that is generally inconsistent with an independent contractor relationship. Independent contractors typically do not have the authority to give direction and control in the way this amendment implies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principles - Control Test for Independent Contractors
perturbation.law_citation: Multiple Common Law Cases (e.g., Stevenson Jordan & Harrison Ltd v Macdonald and Evans [1952] 1 TLR 101)
perturbation.law_explanation: Common law uses the 'control test' to distinguish between employees and independent contractors. The level of control exerted by the hiring party is a key factor. Granting the 'Contractor' authority to provide 'direction and control' strongly suggests an employer-employee relationship, contradicting the stated intention of the agreement and potentially triggering employment law obligations.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'material advice and support' to 'direction and control' directly contradicts the independent contractor relationship initially established in the contract. The Law Explanation clearly states that 'direction and control' implies an employer-employee relationship. This is supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which discusses the common law 'control test' used to differentiate between employees and independent contractors. Granting the Contractor the authority to give direction and control suggests an employer-employee relationship, contradicting the stated intention of the agreement.
